---
title: "bambamR: End-to-End RNA-Seq Processing from FASTQ to Publication-Ready Plots"
author: "Raban Heller, Hanno Witte, Konrad Steinestel — BwKrhs Ulm"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{bambamR: End-to-End RNA-Seq Processing from FASTQ to Publication-Ready Plots}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.width = 6.5,
  fig.height = 4.5,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "90%",
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE
)
```

```{r logo, echo = FALSE, out.width = "150px", fig.align = "right"}
knitr::include_graphics("../man/figures/logo.png")
```

# Introduction

**bambamR** is an R package for end-to-end RNA-seq analysis. It covers the
full pipeline from raw FASTQ/BAM files through quality control, alignment,
read counting, normalization, differential expression analysis, and
publication-ready visualizations including onco plots, volcano plots,
heatmaps, PCA, and MA plots.

## Key Design Principles

- **Graceful degradation**: All Bioconductor dependencies are optional.
  The package operates in *minimal mode* (CRAN-only) or *full mode*
  (with Bioconductor). It never breaks if Bioconductor is missing.
- **Consistent interface**: All exported functions use the `bb_` prefix.
  All plot functions return `ggplot2` objects for easy customization.
- **Bundled example data**: Every feature can be explored without
  downloading external files or installing Bioconductor.

## Minimal vs. Full Mode

| Feature | Minimal (CRAN) | Full (+ Bioconductor) |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| CPM / TPM normalization | Yes | Yes |
| TMM / RLE normalization | -- | Yes |
| DESeq2 / edgeR / limma | -- | Yes |
| PCA, volcano, heatmap, MA | Yes | Yes |
| Onco plots | Yes | Yes |
| FASTQ / BAM import | Base-R | ShortRead / Rsamtools |
| Alignment wrappers | Yes | Yes |
| Read counting | featureCounts CLI | GenomicAlignments |
| Shiny interactive app | Yes | Yes |

# Installation

Install from CRAN (when available):

```{r install-cran, eval = FALSE}
install.packages("bambamR")
```

Install the development version from GitHub:

```{r install-github, eval = FALSE}
pak::pak("r-heller/bambamR")
```

Optionally install Bioconductor packages for full-mode features:

```{r install-bioc, eval = FALSE}
BiocManager::install(c(
  "DESeq2", "edgeR", "limma",
  "ShortRead", "Rsamtools",
  "GenomicAlignments", "GenomicRanges"
))
```

```{r load-library}
library(bambamR)
```

---

# Bundled Example Data

bambamR ships with ready-to-use datasets accessible via convenience
functions:

| Function | Contents |
|---|---|
| `bb_example_counts()` | 200 genes x 10 samples + metadata |
| `bb_example_mutations()` | 300 mutations, 50 samples, 20 genes + clinical data |
| `bb_example_de()` | 500-gene pre-computed DE results |

```{r load-data}
# RNA-seq count matrix (200 genes x 10 samples)
ex <- bb_example_counts()
counts   <- ex$counts
metadata <- ex$metadata
```

```{r show-counts}
dim(counts)
counts[1:5, 1:5]
```

```{r show-metadata}
metadata
```

---

# Normalization

## CPM (Counts Per Million)

CPM normalization works without any optional dependencies:

```{r normalize-cpm}
cpm <- bb_normalize(counts, method = "cpm")
```

## TPM (Transcripts Per Million)

TPM requires gene lengths:

```{r normalize-tpm}
gene_lengths <- readRDS(
  system.file("extdata", "example_gene_lengths.rds", package = "bambamR")
)
tpm <- bb_normalize(counts, method = "tpm",
                    gene_lengths = gene_lengths$length)
```

## TMM and RLE (Bioconductor)

With Bioconductor installed:

```{r normalize-bioc, eval = FALSE}
tmm <- bb_normalize(counts, method = "tmm")  # requires edgeR
rle <- bb_normalize(counts, method = "rle")  # requires DESeq2
```

---

# Visualization

All plot functions return `ggplot2` objects that can be further
customized with `+ theme()`, `+ labs()`, etc.

## PCA Plot

```{r pca, fig.cap = "PCA plot colored by experimental condition."}
bb_pca(cpm, metadata, color_by = "condition")
```

```{r pca-batch, fig.cap = "PCA plot with shape mapping for batch effect."}
bb_pca(cpm, metadata, color_by = "condition",
       shape_by = "batch", label = TRUE, point_size = 4)
```

---

## Heatmap

```{r heatmap-var, fig.cap = "Heatmap of the 30 most variable genes.", fig.height = 6}
bb_heatmap(cpm, n_genes = 30)
```

---

## Volcano Plot

Using the pre-computed DE results (no Bioconductor required):

```{r de-load}
de_results <- bb_example_de()
sig <- sum(de_results$padj < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE)
cat("Significant genes (FDR < 0.05):", sig, "\n")
```

```{r volcano, fig.cap = "Volcano plot highlighting differentially expressed genes."}
bb_volcano(de_results, fc_cutoff = 1, p_cutoff = 0.05, n_label = 8)
```

---

```{r volcano-custom, fig.cap = "Volcano plot with custom gene labels and colors."}
bb_volcano(de_results,
           fc_cutoff = 0.5,
           p_cutoff = 0.01,
           label_genes = c("TP53", "KRAS", "EGFR", "PTEN"),
           colors = c(up = "#E41A1C", down = "#377EB8", ns = "grey80"))
```

## MA Plot

```{r ma-plot, fig.cap = "MA plot showing fold-change vs.~mean expression."}
bb_ma_plot(de_results, p_cutoff = 0.05)
```

---

## Heatmap of Top DE Genes

```{r heatmap-de, fig.cap = "Heatmap of the top 25 differentially expressed genes.", fig.height = 6}
bb_heatmap(cpm, de_result = de_results, n_genes = 25)
```

---

# Oncoplot

The `bb_oncoplot()` function creates publication-ready waterfall-style
mutation landscape plots.

## Data Format

bambamR accepts a `data.frame` with columns `sample`, `gene`, and
`mutation_type`, or MAF-format data with `Hugo_Symbol`,
`Tumor_Sample_Barcode`, and `Variant_Classification`.

```{r oncoplot-data}
mut <- bb_example_mutations()
head(mut$mutations)
```

## Basic Oncoplot

```{r oncoplot-basic, fig.cap = "Oncoplot showing the top 10 mutated genes.", fig.width = 7, fig.height = 5}
bb_oncoplot(mut$mutations, n_genes = 10, show_barplot = FALSE)
```

---

## Oncoplot with Clinical Annotations

```{r oncoplot-clinical, fig.cap = "Oncoplot with clinical annotation tracks (Stage, Gender, Smoking).", fig.width = 7, fig.height = 5}
bb_oncoplot(mut$mutations, n_genes = 8,
            annotation_df = mut$clinical, show_barplot = FALSE)
```

## Selecting Specific Genes

```{r oncoplot-genes, fig.cap = "Oncoplot showing five selected genes.", fig.width = 7, fig.height = 4}
bb_oncoplot(mut$mutations, show_barplot = FALSE,
            genes = c("TP53", "KRAS", "PIK3CA", "BRAF", "EGFR"))
```

## Custom Colors

```{r oncoplot-colors, fig.cap = "Oncoplot with a custom color palette.", fig.width = 7, fig.height = 5}
my_colors <- c(
  "Missense_Mutation" = "#3182BD",
  "Nonsense_Mutation" = "#E6550D",
  "Frame_Shift_Del"   = "#31A354",
  "Frame_Shift_Ins"   = "#756BB1",
  "Splice_Site"       = "#DE2D26",
  "In_Frame_Del"      = "#636363",
  "In_Frame_Ins"      = "#FDAE6B",
  "Translation_Start_Site" = "#BCBDDC",
  "Multi_Hit"         = "#FDD0A2",
  "Other"             = "#BDBDBD"
)
bb_oncoplot(mut$mutations, n_genes = 8,
            mutation_colors = my_colors, show_barplot = FALSE)
```

---

# FASTQ Import

bambamR includes a small example FASTQ file (100 reads, 75 bp):

```{r fastq-import}
fq_path <- system.file("extdata", "example_reads.fastq",
                        package = "bambamR")
reads <- bb_read_fastq(fq_path, n = 5)
reads
```

When `ShortRead` is available, `bb_read_fastq()` uses it automatically.
Otherwise, a base-R parser handles plain and gzipped FASTQ files.

# Quality Control

```{r qc}
qc <- bb_qc(fastq_path = fq_path)
qc
```

```{r qc-summary}
bb_qc_summary(qc)
```

```{r qc-plot, fig.cap = "QC visualization panel: per-position quality, GC content, and read length distribution.", fig.height = 8}
bb_plot_qc(qc)
```

---

# Differential Expression

Three DE methods are supported, each requiring an optional Bioconductor
package. All three return a standardized `data.frame` with columns
`gene`, `log2fc`, `pvalue`, `padj`, and optionally `basemean`.

```{r de-methods, eval = FALSE}
# DESeq2 (requires DESeq2)
de <- bb_deseq2(counts, metadata)

# edgeR (requires edgeR)
de <- bb_edger(counts, group = metadata$condition)

# limma-voom (requires limma + edgeR)
design <- model.matrix(~ condition, data = metadata)
de <- bb_limma_voom(counts, design)
```

The standardized output ensures that `bb_volcano()`, `bb_ma_plot()`,
`bb_heatmap()`, and `bb_export_csv()` work identically regardless of
the DE method used.

# Export

```{r export, eval = FALSE}
bb_export_csv(de_results, "de_results.csv")
bb_export_tsv(de_results, "de_results.tsv")
bb_export_rds(de_results, "de_results.rds")
```

---

# Full Pipeline

The `bb_pipeline()` function runs the entire analysis end-to-end.
It accepts entry at three stages:

```{r pipeline-diagram, eval = FALSE}
# From FASTQ files
result <- bb_pipeline(
  fastq_dir    = "raw_reads/",
  genome_index = "STAR_index/",
  annotation   = "genes.gtf",
  sample_info  = metadata,
  aligner      = "STAR",
  de_method    = "DESeq2",
  threads      = 8
)

# From BAM files (skip alignment)
result <- bb_pipeline(
  bam_dir     = "aligned/",
  annotation  = "genes.gtf",
  sample_info = metadata
)

# From a count matrix (skip alignment + counting)
result <- bb_pipeline(
  count_matrix = my_counts,
  sample_info  = metadata,
  de_method    = "edgeR"
)
```

The returned `bb_result` object bundles counts, metadata, DE results,
and all generated plots:

```{r pipeline-result, eval = FALSE}
result$counts          # count matrix
result$de_results      # standardized DE data.frame
result$plots$pca       # ggplot object
result$plots$volcano   # ggplot object
result$plots$heatmap   # ggplot object
```

# Interactive Shiny App

Launch a browser-based dashboard for interactive analysis:

```{r shiny, eval = FALSE}
bb_run_app()
```

The app provides:

- Data upload (CSV, TSV, RDS) with live preview
- Configurable normalization and DE parameters
- Interactive plots (volcano, PCA, heatmap, MA, oncoplot)
- One-click export (CSV, RDS, PDF)

---

# Function Reference

### Import

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `bb_read_fastq()` | Read FASTQ files |
| `bb_read_bam()` | Read BAM files |
| `bb_count_bam()` | Count reads in BAM |

### Quality Control

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `bb_qc()` | Compute QC metrics |
| `bb_qc_summary()` | Tabular QC summary |
| `bb_plot_qc()` | QC visualization panel |

### Alignment & Counting

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `bb_align()` | Align with STAR / HISAT2 / minimap2 |
| `bb_count_reads()` | Count reads per gene |

### Normalization

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `bb_normalize()` | CPM, TPM, TMM, or RLE |

### Differential Expression

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `bb_deseq2()` | DESeq2 wrapper |
| `bb_edger()` | edgeR quasi-likelihood wrapper |
| `bb_limma_voom()` | limma-voom wrapper |

### Visualization

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `bb_oncoplot()` | Waterfall mutation plot |
| `bb_volcano()` | Volcano plot |
| `bb_heatmap()` | Clustered heatmap |
| `bb_pca()` | PCA plot |
| `bb_ma_plot()` | MA plot |

### Pipeline & Export

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `bb_pipeline()` | Full end-to-end pipeline |
| `bb_export_csv()` | Export as CSV |
| `bb_export_tsv()` | Export as TSV |
| `bb_export_rds()` | Export as RDS |

### Example Data & App

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `bb_example_counts()` | Example count matrix |
| `bb_example_mutations()` | Example mutation data |
| `bb_example_de()` | Example DE results |
| `bb_run_app()` | Launch Shiny dashboard |

# Citation

If you use bambamR in your research, please cite:

> Heller R, Witte H, Steinestel K (2026). *bambamR: End-to-End RNA-Seq
> Processing from FASTQ to Publication-Ready Plots.* R package version
> 0.1.0. https://github.com/r-heller/bambamR

# Session Info

```{r session-info}
sessionInfo()
```
